does: This paper introduces Mobius Injection, a novel attack vector that exploits a structural vulnerability called Semantic Closure in autonomous LLM agents to launch AbO-DDoS (Agent-Based and -Oriented DDoS) attacks. The attack leverages a single textual injection to trigger sustained recursive execution of agent components, causing the agent to become a distributed denial-of-service weapon against AI infrastructure. The authors demonstrate that this attack is lightweight, evades traditional DDoS monitoring systems and contemporary AI safety filters, and allows for surgical targeting of specific environments or model providers, representing a previously overlooked systemic risk in the agent-user-service infrastructure chain.
connects: This work builds on growing research into LLM security vulnerabilities, extending beyond isolated model attacks to the systemic risks posed by autonomous agents acting as intermediaries in complex service ecosystems. It combines insights from adversarial prompt injection research, agent orchestration systems, and DDoS attack methodologies to expose how structural properties of agentic logic can be weaponized. The paper responds to the gap in prior security research that has focused on agent safety in isolation while neglecting the broader infrastructure-level risks that emerge when agents are deployed as coordinators between users and services, particularly addressing how recursive reasoning loops in agent execution can be exploited.
enables: This research points toward several urgent follow-up directions including the design of architectural safeguards that prevent Semantic Closure exploitation in agent systems, development of monitoring systems specifically tuned to detect recursive injection patterns, and new standards for agentic infrastructure resilience. It motivates the need for principled frameworks for reasoning about systemic risks in multi-agent and agent-service chains, rather than treating security in isolation. The work also enables a deeper investigation into how autonomous agents amplify attack surface area and suggests the need for rate-limiting, execution quotas, and formal verification methods for agent logic to prevent weaponization of trusted intermediaries in digital infrastructure.
